Problem Statement: For each of the following series, if the divergence test applies, either state that $$\underset{n \rightarrow \infty}{\text{lim}} a_{n}$$ does not exist or find $$\underset{n \rightarrow \infty}{\text{lim}} a_{n} .$$ If the divergence test does not apply, state why.
Question: $$a_{n} = \frac{n}{n + 2}$$

Problem Statement: For each of the following series, if the divergence test applies, either state that $$\underset{n \rightarrow \infty}{\text{lim}} a_{n}$$ does not exist or find $$\underset{n \rightarrow \infty}{\text{lim}} a_{n} .$$ If the divergence test does not apply, state why.
Question: $$a_{n} = \frac{n}{5 n^{2} - 3}$$
Answer: $$\underset{n \rightarrow \infty}{\text{lim}} a_{n} = 0 .$$ Divergence test does not apply.

Problem Statement: For each of the following series, if the divergence test applies, either state that $$\underset{n \rightarrow \infty}{\text{lim}} a_{n}$$ does not exist or find $$\underset{n \rightarrow \infty}{\text{lim}} a_{n} .$$ If the divergence test does not apply, state why.
Question: $$a_{n} = \text{tan} n$$
Answer: $$\underset{n \rightarrow \infty}{\text{lim}} a_{n}$$ does not exist. Series diverges.

Problem Statement: For each of the following series, if the divergence test applies, either state that $$\underset{n \rightarrow \infty}{\text{lim}} a_{n}$$ does not exist or find $$\underset{n \rightarrow \infty}{\text{lim}} a_{n} .$$ If the divergence test does not apply, state why.

Question: $$a_{n} = \frac{( \text{ln} n )^{2}}{\sqrt{n}}$$
Answer: $$\underset{n \rightarrow \infty}{\text{lim}} a_{n} = 0 .$$ Divergence test does not apply.

Problem Statement: State whether the given $$p$$ -series converges.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}$$

Problem Statement: State whether the given $$p$$ -series converges.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n \sqrt{n}}$$

Problem Statement: State whether the given $$p$$ -series converges.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sqrt[3]{n^{2}}}$$

Problem Statement: State whether the given $$p$$ -series converges.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sqrt[3]{n^{4}}}$$

Problem Statement: State whether the given $$p$$ -series converges.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{n^{e}}{n^{\pi}}$$

Problem Statement: State whether the given $$p$$ -series converges.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{n^{\pi}}{n^{2 e}}$$

Problem Statement: Use the integral test to determine whether the following sums converge.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sqrt{n + 5}}$$

Problem Statement: Use the integral test to determine whether the following sums converge.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sqrt[3]{n + 5}}$$
Answer: Series diverges by comparison with $$\int_{1}^{\infty} \frac{d x}{( x + 5 )^{1 \text{/} 3}} .$$

Problem Statement: Use the integral test to determine whether the following sums converge.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 2}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n \text{ln} n}$$

Problem Statement: Use the integral test to determine whether the following sums converge.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{n}{1 + n^{2}}$$
Answer: Series diverges by comparison with $$\int_{1}^{\infty} \frac{x}{1 + x^{2}} d x .$$

Problem Statement: Use the integral test to determine whether the following sums converge.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{e^{n}}{1 + e^{2 n}}$$

Problem Statement: Use the integral test to determine whether the following sums converge.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{2 n}{1 + n^{4}}$$
Answer: Series converges by comparison with $$\int_{1}^{\infty} \frac{2 x}{1 + x^{4}} d x .$$

Problem Statement: Use the integral test to determine whether the following sums converge.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 2}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n \text{ln}^{2} n}$$

Problem Statement: Express the following sums as $$p$$ -series and determine whether each converges.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} 2^{\text{unicode[:-]} \text{ln} n}$$ ( Hint: $$2^{\text{unicode[:-]} \text{ln} n} = 1 \text{/} n^{\text{ln} 2}$$ .)

Problem Statement: Express the following sums as $$p$$ -series and determine whether each converges.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} 3^{\text{unicode[:-]} \text{ln} n}$$ ( Hint: $$3^{\text{unicode[:-]} \text{ln} n} = 1 \text{/} n^{\text{ln} 3}$$ .)

Problem Statement: Express the following sums as $$p$$ -series and determine whether each converges.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} n 2^{-2 \text{ln} n}$$

Problem Statement: Express the following sums as $$p$$ -series and determine whether each converges.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} n 3^{-2 \text{ln} n}$$

Problem Statement: Use the estimate $$R_{N} \le \int_{N}^{\infty} f ( t ) d t$$ to find a bound for the remainder $$R_{N} = \sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} a_{n} - \sum_{n = 1}^{N} a_{n}$$ where $$a_{n} = f ( n ) .$$
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{1000} \frac{1}{n^{2}}$$

Problem Statement: Use the estimate $$R_{N} \le \int_{N}^{\infty} f ( t ) d t$$ to find a bound for the remainder $$R_{N} = \sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} a_{n} - \sum_{n = 1}^{N} a_{n}$$ where $$a_{n} = f ( n ) .$$
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{1000} \frac{1}{n^{3}}$$

Problem Statement: Use the estimate $$R_{N} \le \int_{N}^{\infty} f ( t ) d t$$ to find a bound for the remainder $$R_{N} = \sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} a_{n} - \sum_{n = 1}^{N} a_{n}$$ where $$a_{n} = f ( n ) .$$
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{1000} \frac{1}{1 + n^{2}}$$

Problem Statement: Use the estimate $$R_{N} \le \int_{N}^{\infty} f ( t ) d t$$ to find a bound for the remainder $$R_{N} = \sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} a_{n} - \sum_{n = 1}^{N} a_{n}$$ where $$a_{n} = f ( n ) .$$
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{100} n \text{/} 2^{n}$$

Problem Statement: [T] Find the minimum value of $$N$$ such that the remainder estimate $$\int_{N + 1}^{\infty} f < R_{N} < \int_{N}^{\infty} f$$ guarantees that $$\sum_{n = 1}^{N} a_{n}$$ estimates $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} a_{n} ,$$ accurate to within the given error.
Question: $$a_{n} = \frac{1}{n^{2}} ,$$ error $$< 10^{-4}$$

Problem Statement: [T] Find the minimum value of $$N$$ such that the remainder estimate $$\int_{N + 1}^{\infty} f < R_{N} < \int_{N}^{\infty} f$$ guarantees that $$\sum_{n = 1}^{N} a_{n}$$ estimates $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} a_{n} ,$$ accurate to within the given error.
Question: $$a_{n} = \frac{1}{n^{1.1}} ,$$ error $$< 10^{-4}$$

Problem Statement: [T] Find the minimum value of $$N$$ such that the remainder estimate $$\int_{N + 1}^{\infty} f < R_{N} < \int_{N}^{\infty} f$$ guarantees that $$\sum_{n = 1}^{N} a_{n}$$ estimates $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} a_{n} ,$$ accurate to within the given error.
Question: $$a_{n} = \frac{1}{n^{1.01}} ,$$ error $$< 10^{-4}$$

Problem Statement: [T] Find the minimum value of $$N$$ such that the remainder estimate $$\int_{N + 1}^{\infty} f < R_{N} < \int_{N}^{\infty} f$$ guarantees that $$\sum_{n = 1}^{N} a_{n}$$ estimates $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} a_{n} ,$$ accurate to within the given error.
Question: $$a_{n} = \frac{1}{n \text{ln}^{2} n} ,$$ error $$< 10^{-3}$$

Problem Statement: [T] Find the minimum value of $$N$$ such that the remainder estimate $$\int_{N + 1}^{\infty} f < R_{N} < \int_{N}^{\infty} f$$ guarantees that $$\sum_{n = 1}^{N} a_{n}$$ estimates $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} a_{n} ,$$ accurate to within the given error.
Question: $$a_{n} = \frac{1}{1 + n^{2}} ,$$ error $$< 10^{-3}$$

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, find a value of $$N$$ such that $$R_{N}$$ is smaller than the desired error. Compute the corresponding sum $$\sum_{n = 1}^{N} a_{n}$$ and compare it to the given estimate of the infinite series.
Question: $$a_{n} = \frac{1}{n^{11}} ,$$ error $$< 10^{-4} ,$$ $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^{11}} = 1.000494 \text{unicode[:ddot.]}$$

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, find a value of $$N$$ such that $$R_{N}$$ is smaller than the desired error. Compute the corresponding sum $$\sum_{n = 1}^{N} a_{n}$$ and compare it to the given estimate of the infinite series.
Question: $$a_{n} = \frac{1}{e^{n}} ,$$ error $$< 10^{-5} ,$$ $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{e^{n}} = \frac{1}{e - 1} = 0.581976 \text{unicode[:ddot.]}$$
Answer: $$R_{N} < \int_{N}^{\infty} \frac{d x}{e^{x}} = e^{\text{unicode[:-]} N} , N > 5 \text{ln} ( 10 ) ,$$ okay if $$N = 12 ; \sum_{n = 1}^{12} e^{\text{unicode[:-]} n} = 0.581973... .$$ Estimate agrees with $$1 \text{/} ( e - 1 )$$ to five decimal places.

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, find a value of $$N$$ such that $$R_{N}$$ is smaller than the desired error. Compute the corresponding sum $$\sum_{n = 1}^{N} a_{n}$$ and compare it to the given estimate of the infinite series.
Question: $$a_{n} = \frac{1}{e^{n^{2}}} ,$$ error $$< 10^{-5} ,$$ $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} n \text{/} e^{n 2} = 0.40488139857 \text{unicode[:ddot.]}$$

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, find a value of $$N$$ such that $$R_{N}$$ is smaller than the desired error. Compute the corresponding sum $$\sum_{n = 1}^{N} a_{n}$$ and compare it to the given estimate of the infinite series.
Question: $$a_{n} = 1 \text{/} n^{4} ,$$ error $$< 10^{-4} ,$$ $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} 1 \text{/} n^{4} = \pi^{4} \text{/} 90 = 1.08232 ...$$
Answer: $$R_{N} < \int_{N}^{\infty} d x \text{/} x^{4} = 1 \text{/} 3 N^{3} , N > ( 10^{4} / 3 )^{1 \text{/} 3} ,$$ okay if $$N = 15 ;$$ $$\sum_{n = 1}^{15} 1 \text{/} n^{4} = 1.08226 \text{unicode[:ddot.]} .$$ Estimate agrees with the sum to three decimal places.

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, find a value of $$N$$ such that $$R_{N}$$ is smaller than the desired error. Compute the corresponding sum $$\sum_{n = 1}^{N} a_{n}$$ and compare it to the given estimate of the infinite series.
Question: $$a_{n} = 1 \text{/} n^{6} ,$$ error $$< 10^{-6} ,$$ $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} 1 \text{/} n^{4} = \pi^{6} \text{/} 945 = 1.01734306... ,$$

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, find a value of $$N$$ such that $$R_{N}$$ is smaller than the desired error. Compute the corresponding sum $$\sum_{n = 1}^{N} a_{n}$$ and compare it to the given estimate of the infinite series.
Question: Find the limit as $$n \vec{} \infty$$ of $$\frac{1}{n} + \frac{1}{n + 1} + \text{cdots} + \frac{1}{2 n} .$$ ( Hint: Compare to $$\int_{n}^{2 n} \frac{1}{t} d t . \text{)}$$

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, find a value of $$N$$ such that $$R_{N}$$ is smaller than the desired error. Compute the corresponding sum $$\sum_{n = 1}^{N} a_{n}$$ and compare it to the given estimate of the infinite series.
Question: Find the limit as $$n \vec{} \infty$$ of $$\frac{1}{n} + \frac{1}{n + 1} + \text{cdots} + \frac{1}{3 n}$$

Problem Statement: The next few exercises are intended to give a sense of applications in which partial sums of the harmonic series arise.
Question: In certain applications of probability, such as the so-called Watterson estimator for predicting mutation rates in population genetics, it is important to have an accurate estimate of the number $$H_{k} = ( 1 + \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{3} + \text{cdots} + \frac{1}{k} ) .$$ Recall that $$T_{k} = H_{k} - \text{ln} k$$ is decreasing. Compute $$T = \underset{k \rightarrow \infty}{\text{lim}} T_{k}$$ to four decimal places. ( Hint: $$\frac{1}{k + 1} < \int_{k}^{k + 1} \frac{1}{x} d x$$ .)

Problem Statement: The next few exercises are intended to give a sense of applications in which partial sums of the harmonic series arise.
Question: [T] Complete sampling with replacement, sometimes called the coupon collector’s problem , is phrased as follows: Suppose you have $$N$$ unique items in a bin. At each step, an item is chosen at random, identified, and put back in the bin. The problem asks what is the expected number of steps $$E ( N )$$ that it takes to draw each unique item at least once. It turns out that $$E ( N ) = N$$ . $$H_{N} = N ( 1 + \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{3} + \text{cdots} + \frac{1}{N} )$$ . Find $$E ( N )$$ for $$N = 10 , 20 , \text{and} 50$$ .

Problem Statement: The next few exercises are intended to give a sense of applications in which partial sums of the harmonic series arise.
Question: [T] The simplest way to shuffle cards is to take the top card and insert it at a random place in the deck, called top random insertion, and then repeat. We will consider a deck to be randomly shuffled once enough top random insertions have been made that the card originally at the bottom has reached the top and then been randomly inserted. If the deck has $$n$$ cards, then the probability that the insertion will be below the card initially at the bottom (call this card $$B )$$ is $$1 \text{/} n .$$ Thus the expected number of top random insertions before $$B$$ is no longer at the bottom is n . Once one card is below $$B ,$$ there are two places below $$B$$ and the probability that a randomly inserted card will fall below $$B$$ is $$2 \text{/} n .$$ The expected number of top random insertions before this happens is $$n \text{/} 2 .$$ The two cards below $$B$$ are now in random order. Continuing this way, find a formula for the expected number of top random insertions needed to consider the deck to be randomly shuffled.
Answer: The expected number of random insertions to get $$B$$ to the top is $$n + n \text{/} 2 + n \text{/} 3 + \text{cdots} + n \text{/} ( n - 1 ) .$$ Then one more insertion puts $$B$$ back in at random. Thus, the expected number of shuffles to randomize the deck is $$n ( 1 + 1 \text{/} 2 + \text{cdots} + 1 \text{/} n ) .$$

Problem Statement: The next few exercises are intended to give a sense of applications in which partial sums of the harmonic series arise.
Question: Suppose a scooter can travel $$100$$ km on a full tank of fuel. Assuming that fuel can be transferred from one scooter to another but can only be carried in the tank, present a procedure that will enable one of the scooters to travel $$100 H_{N}$$ km, where $$H_{N} = 1 + 1 \text{/} 2 + \text{cdots} + 1 \text{/} N .$$

Problem Statement: The next few exercises are intended to give a sense of applications in which partial sums of the harmonic series arise.
Question: Show that for the remainder estimate to apply on $$[ N , \infty )$$ it is sufficient that $$f ( x )$$ be decreasing on $$[ N , \infty ) ,$$ but $$f$$ need not be decreasing on $$[ 1 , \infty ) .$$
Answer: Set $$b_{n} = a_{n + N}$$ and $$g ( t ) = f ( t + N )$$ such that $$f$$ is decreasing on $$[ t , \infty ) .$$

Problem Statement: The next few exercises are intended to give a sense of applications in which partial sums of the harmonic series arise.
Question: [T] Use the remainder estimate and integration by parts to approximate $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} n \text{/} e^{n}$$ within an error smaller than $$0.0001 .$$

Problem Statement: The next few exercises are intended to give a sense of applications in which partial sums of the harmonic series arise.
Question: Does $$\sum_{n = 2}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n ( \text{ln} n )^{p}}$$ converge if $$p$$ is large enough? If so, for which $$p \text{?}$$
Answer: The series converges for $$p > 1$$ by integral test using change of variable.

Problem Statement: The next few exercises are intended to give a sense of applications in which partial sums of the harmonic series arise.
Question: [T] Suppose a computer can sum one million terms per second of the divergent series $$\sum_{n = 1}^{N} \frac{1}{n} .$$ Use the integral test to approximate how many seconds it will take to add up enough terms for the partial sum to exceed $$100 .$$

Problem Statement: The next few exercises are intended to give a sense of applications in which partial sums of the harmonic series arise.
Question: [T] A fast computer can sum one million terms per second of the divergent series $$\sum_{n = 2}^{N} \frac{1}{n \text{ln} n} .$$ Use the integral test to approximate how many seconds it will take to add up enough terms for the partial sum to exceed $$100 .$$
